input.question: We are a student engineering team designing a vehicle for the FSAE competition. Attached is the FSAE rules document. Also attached is an image that shows an engineering drawing of the vehicle on the top accompanied by six CAD views of the vehicle on the bottom. The six CAD views each feature a different orientation of our design, so that 3D information about our design can be inferred. The CAD views are provided to contextualize the engineering drawing, which has the same orientation as one of the six CAD views. All units displayed in the engineering drawing have units of mm. Based on the engineering drawing, does our design comply with rule T.7.6.1 specified in the FSAE rule document? To answer the question, use the scale bar shown at the top of the engineering drawing to compute necessary dimensions in the drawing.  First provide an explanation for your answer (begin it with 'Explanation:'). Then provide just a yes/no answer (begin it with 'Answer:') that summarizes your response.

retrieved_context:
RULE: t.7.6.1
            TITLE: when forward of the centerline of the front wheel axles:

            CONTENT:
            rule T.7.6.1: When forward of the centerline of the front wheel axles:
T.7.6.1 When forward of the centerline of the front wheel axles:   
Inboard of two vertical planes parallel to the centerline of the chassis touching the outside of 
the front tires at the height of the hubs.

final_answer:
Explanation:Rule T.7.6.1 mandates that the width of the vehicle, when measured forward of the centerline of the front wheel axles, must be contained inboard of two vertical planes parallel to the chassis centerline that touch the outside of the front tires at the height of the hubs. This effectively means the maximum width of the bodywork in the front section must not exceed the track width (the distance between the outside edges of the front tires). Observing the provided top-down plan view, the bodywork structure extending forward of the front wheel axles is visually contained within the width defined by the front tires. Therefore, the design appears to comply with this rule.
Answer:Yes
